content: It is a very good idea for a clash. It is nice to have a story. There is just one little point, which is I found there are a bit too much of tests. For a speed challenge, it could be better to have just 10 tests, to make debuging faster. But continue like this, it is very good :)

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: In the range of the right level of difficulty for a clash

I thought you'd be more patient than the goblins and wait for my review first before publishing the contribution 😆

Stub generator
* Please revise Line 2 to use loopline instead of read and int instead of string.

Cases
* Please add at least one pair of simple cases and one pair of complex cases (you can add more and/or modify the existing ones too if you want, but please include at least two pairs) where some goblins leave the queue because their patience runs out, but later goblins (who would otherwise have left) end up being served just in time.

Here's a simple example:

5
0 2 3 1 3

In this case, the last goblin would have left, but because the goblin before him leaves first, the last goblin ends up being served in time.

If my interpretation is incorrect (for example, regarding how the queue should be simulated), please let me know.

Thanks.
😆 I was thirsty! But now that I'm drunk, I need to rest for an hour or two. I'll be back asap! Thanks for reviewing, as always :)
And fun! Thanks for the Clash.

Well, I lost my patience and left the queue! :)
I added the tests “Thanks to the Complainer! ” and “Clearing the Path!”. I don't think any other tests are really necessary, but let me know. Your interpretation is absolutely correct!

(Speaking of tests, is there any way to insert a test among others? Or upload a JSON or something? :p)

Great! Just one more fix:

In the stub generator, please change "x" to "patience" 😉

As for updating a contribution, you might be able to prepare the modified cases offline and then upload them using one of the APIs. I haven't personally tried this approach, so you may need to experiment a bit.

A user once described a possible procedure in CG's Discord chat:

1. Open the browser's DevTools
2. Make a change to the contribution and save it (this is just to trigger the API).
3. Locate the request to /services/Contribution/updateContribution and copy the request body.
4. Edit the body in any text editor.
5. Send a new request with the modified body.

After that, only steps 4 and 5 should be necessary to repeat the procedure for this or other contributions in the future, unless there's a parameter that can't be obtained without first triggering the API to retrieve it.

You may also find the following reference helpful, as it describes a similar procedure for a different API: https://www.codingame.com/playgrounds/53705/contest-tools-and-workflow/the-codingame-api
Wow! nice! Thanks for those info!! :)
